paper_text:
# Mass as Curvature: a representation of the SM fermion sector

**Status:** working document, single canonical source.
**Date:** 2026-04-14 (reframed as a representation; citations added; σ language removed; review-driven cleanup pass)

---

## A note to reviewers on what this document is

**This is a research note, not a manuscript.** It is a discussion
draft — the kind of document one writes to float an idea to
colleagues, not the kind one submits to a journal. It has not
been through the self-review pass that would precede a formal
submission; several methodological obligations are explicitly
listed under "Prerequisites for external publication" below as
things the author intends to discharge *before* any such
submission, and knows have not been discharged yet.

The useful kind of feedback on a document at this stage is:

- **Large structural holes.** Is there a load-bearing move that
  is just wrong? A claim that contradicts itself between
  sections? An assumption that quietly does all the work and
  isn't acknowledged?
- **Framing problems.** Is the epistemic stance
  ("representation, not theory") coherent, or is it being used
  to dodge something it shouldn't? Is the distinction between
  empirical observation, algebraic consequence, and speculation
  drawn clearly enough that a careful reader can tell which is
  which on each page?
- **Prior-art collisions.** Has somebody already done this, or
  done something close enough that the framing needs to be
  adjusted? The citations are sparse and partially from memory.
- **Obvious dead ends.** Is there a reason this whole line of
  thinking can't work that the author is missing? Pushback of
  the form "you can't do X because of Y" is the most valuable
  kind.
- **Places where the "it's just a representation" move is
  doing too much work.** If the disclaimer is protecting a
  claim that really does need to be defended, call it out.

The *less* useful kind of feedback, at this stage, is
full-academic-rigor review: "you didn't propagate α_s
uncertainties through CRunDec," "you didn't run a null-model
coincidence-frequency estimate," "you didn't enumerate all
structural degrees of freedom." These are all real obligations
and they are all already listed in the prerequisites section.
Flagging them again in review produces a correctly-populated
worklist but not new information. The author already knows.

What the author does *not* already know is whether the central
idea — lepton curvature as a coordinate, the two-road structure,
charm as corner building — is wrong in a way that reading more
carefully would reveal, or whether the framing is confused in a
way that only an outside reader can see. Those are the things
this draft is being floated to find out.

Everything below is written at working-note register: more
informal than a paper, more precise than a conversation,
deliberately incomplete where full rigor would be expensive and
premature. Read accordingly.

---

## The Japan address problem

Japanese addresses don't name streets. They name blocks. The roads
between blocks are the unnamed spaces. To find a building in Japan
you walk down a hierarchical drill-down: country, then prefecture,
then city, then ward, then block, then a house number. House
numbers within a block aren't in spatial order; they're assigned
in the order the houses were built. From outside the system this
looks random. From inside it is perfectly complete: every building
has an address, and every address locates exactly one building.

The author has lived in Japan since 2010 — over fifteen years at
time of writing — and the observation that this is the inverse of
the Western system has been sitting around for a while. (Derek
Sivers makes essentially the same observation in his 2009 TED talk
*Weird, or just different?* as a parallel articulation. His
closing line is the one that matters: whatever idea you hold, the
opposite may also be true.)

The Standard Model's list of fermion masses is a Japanese address
book in two ways at once:

1. **It names the buildings, not the roads.** Each particle
   carries its own value (m_e, m_μ, m_τ, m_u, m_d, m_s, m_c, m_b,
   m_t, plus three neutrino masses). The relationships between
   these values — the structural roads running between them — are
   not part of the standard description. You memorize the
   buildings.

2. **The numbering is construction order, not structure.** The
   quark labels u, d, s, c, b, t come from discovery order. Up
   and down from the 1964 quark-parton proposal. Strange from
   the "strange particles" of the 1950s. Charm from the November
   Revolution of 1974. Bottom in 1977, top in 1995. "Generation
   1, 2, 3" is assigned chronologically by when particles were
   first observed, not by any logical criterion tied to their
   properties. These are construction-order house numbers,
   exactly the Japanese pattern.

Both naming systems are complete. They name opposite things.

---

## What this is: a representation

This document proposes a dual description of the same particles —
one that names the roads where the SM names the buildings. **It is
not a theory. It is not a set of predictions. It is not a claim
that the Standard Model is wrong.** It is a representation: a
human-readable rendering of the same underlying physics, organized
so that relationships between particles become visible at a
glance.

The closest analogy is the periodic table. Mendeleev didn't derive
carbon from first principles. He placed it in a grid where its
relationships to neighbors became obvious — why carbon behaves
like silicon, why lithium behaves like sodium, why helium and neon
are inert. The grid itself isn't physical. It's a convention for
drawing the same chemistry in a way that makes patterns navigable.
The SM particle chart isn't physical either. Both are
representations that earn their place by making the underlying
relationships visible without replacing the underlying math.

The framework inverts the usual direction. The standard direction
is: start with the measured fermion masses, look for patterns
among them. That has been done many times — by Koide (1981, 1983)
with the original lepton formula, by Rivero & Gsponer (2005) in
revisiting and reframing it, by Brannen (2006 onward) with the
circulant-matrix representation that exposes the lepton phase
2/9, and by Rivero (2011) and others in attempting heavy-quark
extensions. **This framework runs the same direction backwards.**
It starts with lepton curvature L as a coordinate axis and lets
each particle find a position on that axis. Mass ratios fall out
as differences in position. RG running becomes a correction *from*
a base position — like a street address that acquires a minor
refinement as you approach the actual building.

**The one rule the framework asks you to accept is this: lepton
energies define a position.** Given the three measured lepton
masses, compute L, and use L as a coordinate. Everything else
follows from this one choice plus mathematics already in the
literature: Koide's algebraic relation (Koide 1983); Soddy's
circle theorem in 2D and 3D (Soddy 1936); Kocik's generalized
Descartes theorem and his explicit reading of Koide as a circle
configuration (Kocik 2007, 2012, 2019, 2020); 4-loop QCD
anomalous-dimension running for quark masses (Chetyrkin, Kühn,
Steinhauser, the RunDec / CRunDec lineage from 2000 onward); and
the current lattice averages for quark masses from the FLAG
collaboration. Full references at the end.

The geometric interpretation we use is Apollonian / Soddy circle
packing, following Kocik. **It is not the only possible geometric
projection of the same algebraic content.** The Koide identity is
an algebraic constraint, and any geometry that realizes that
constraint is a candidate projection. Other geometric readings
(linked tori, gasket fractals, spinor configurations) may also
work. We choose this projection because it organizes the data
cleanly and connects to existing literature; we are not claiming
it is the unique one. Geometry is a projection of algebra.

Address systems are tools. Japanese addresses are good for finding
a specific building when you know the prefecture; Western
addresses are good for seeing which buildings sit next to each
other on a street. Neither is "the real geography" — both are
projections that make different things visible, and you can mix
and match freely depending on which question you're asking. The
framework's choice is to **reorder** the SM fermion masses by
magnitude, then ask what connects them, and to use Koide's
relation as the connector because it was already there in the
literature, it is accurate, and it is geometric in a way that
lets you picture and navigate the structure. Reorder, readdress,
look at how the entities are actually connected. **The geometry
simply follows.**

### A note on language: residuals, not significances

Throughout this document we report deviations as **percent
residuals** ("0.10% deviation," "0.51% residual") rather than as
multiples of measurement error ("σ"). This is deliberate. The
framework is an exploratory representational construction over a
high-dimensional space of choices (which particles, which
groupings, which scales, which exponents); residual-vs-error
ratios are not valid p-values in that setting, because the
implicit number of trials is not controlled and there is no
prespecified search protocol. The percent residuals report what
the framework actually says: how close a structural placement
sits to a measured value. Whether that closeness is *evidentially*
meaningful, against a null model of generic numerological
coincidence, is a separate question that this document does not
answer. (See "Prerequisites for external publication" below.)

### What this is doing for the parameter count

If you accept the one rule — that lepton energies can define a
position — the same twelve charged-fermion masses can be described
with a smaller set of explicit inputs: m_e, m_μ, α_s(M_Z),
m_c(m_c), and one heavy-quark anchor (m_b or m_t). The other
masses sit at structural positions in the map and follow from
those inputs plus the math listed above.

**This is compression of the description, not reduction of free
parameters in an underlying theory.** The Standard Model's
parameter count remains what it is. What we have done is
reorganize the same information so that relationships between
particles are visible — without invoking new physics, new forces,
fitted scales, or hidden parameters. The reorganization uses
**measured and RG-evolved masses** (FLAG lattice values, evolved
between scales via 4-loop CRunDec where needed); we
**mass-order them on the map, and the road structure becomes
visible**. Whether this reorganization eventually constrains
flavor-model building, or serves only as a useful navigational
map on its own, we do not claim. We are offering the map.

A note on what "compression" honestly counts: the explicit
numerical inputs are five, but the framework also makes structural
choices (which particles belong on the L-ladder vs. on a Koide
triple; which scale each mass is evaluated at; which Koide
rotation root is taken). These structural choices are not "free
parameters" in the usual sense — they are made once, before
looking at residuals, and are then fixed — but they are also not
nothing. A fully honest parameter accounting for an external
paper would have to enumerate them. This working document does
not yet do so.

### The epistemic stance that follows

- **Is the math already there?** Yes. Koide (1981, 1983), Rivero
  & Gsponer (2005), Brannen (2006), Rivero (2011), Soddy (1936),
  Kocik (2007, 2012, 2019, 2020), the CRunDec lineage, and the
  FLAG lattice averages are all already published. The framework
  arranges these into a single coherent map; it does not
  introduce new mathematics.
- **Does it represent the SM charged fermion masses accurately?**
  Yes — to sub-percent residuals for 8 of the 12 fermion masses.
  Two (the down quark and the neutrino sum) are honestly flagged
  as stubborn.
- **Does it predict new particles?** No. It places the known ones.
- **Is it the correct answer?** Unknown, and "correct" may not
  even be the right question to ask of a representation. Other
  maps may also be valid.
- **Is there math underlying the geometry?** Yes. Geometry is a
  projection of algebra. We use the Apollonian / Soddy projection
  because it organizes the data cleanly; other projections may
  also work.

### A methodological note

This framework is what one sees when approaching the SM mass
sector by **mass-ordering first and labeling second**, rather
than the other way around. The standard pedagogical path teaches
u, d, s, c, b, t and the three generations as primary and the
actual numerical values as secondary metadata — by the time a
physicist is fluent enough to look for structure among the
masses, the discovery-order labels feel natural, the magnitudes
feel like attached annotations, and the question "what do these
numbers actually look like in order" rarely gets asked.

Reversing that order — treating the measured masses as the
primary objects and the labels as construction-order house
numbers attached after the fact — is what makes the road
structure visible. We mention this not as autobiography but as a
note on what the framework is and isn't: it is a structural map
produced by approaching the mass sector with the labels
deliberately demoted, and it may be harder to see if you come at
it with the labels already cemented as primary.

---

## Multiply-connected addresses

Before getting to the math, one feature of the address metaphor
matters for what comes later. In any address system, some
buildings have more than one address. A house on a corner faces
two streets and gets a number on each. A building with a back
door reachable from a different street is findable from both
directions. Both addresses are correct; the address you use
depends on which side you're walking in from.

When we organize the SM charged fermions into the framework's two
road systems, **some particles turn out to be multiply addressed
in this sense**. The L-ladder gives one road, the heavy Koide
triple gives another, and any particle that participates in both
ends up with one address on each.

A consequence of this: the global geometry doesn't have to embed
cleanly in any low number of dimensions. We are not claiming the
road system can be drawn on a single sheet of paper without
overlap, or that the underlying connectivity is geometrically
simple. We are claiming that the **local description** at each
particle works — that you can stand at the position of the tau
lepton and look one street over to the lepton-sum scale, and the
address structure makes sense locally. As more connections are
eventually found, the global picture may need higher dimensions,
or may become physically impossible to embed in any flat
geometry. **That is allowed.** The purpose of an address book is
local navigation, not a globally embeddable map.

The geometric shape improves understanding of the local
neighborhood around each particle. It does not have to be
extendable to a single coherent global picture, and it does not
have to commit to a particular dimensionality of the embedding
space. The framework's claim is local, not global.

This matters for charm in particular. Charm will end up
multiply-addressed in the road system below — once on the
L-ladder and once as a vertex of the heavy Koide triple — and the
framework chooses to describe this as a corner-building pattern.
Both addresses sit at framework-level closeness; neither is
"more real" than the other.

---

## The framework in one paragraph

Define the **lepton seed**

    L  =  √m_e + √m_μ + √m_τ  −  √(m_e + m_μ + m_τ)  ≈  9.7528

This is the outer Soddy curvature of the lepton triple in the
Apollonian-circle reading of Koide's formula (Kocik 2012). Each
particle is assigned a **ladder position** n. For any two
particles X and Y, their mass ratio is

    m_X / m_Y  =  L^(n_X − n_Y)

Both sides of this equation are pure numbers regardless of how
L's units are interpreted. The framework represents particles as
dimensionless ratios; one external anchor (typically m_e in MeV)
fixes the absolute scale.

There are **two road systems**:

1. The **L-ladder**: u, d, s, c each sit at a power of L.
2. The **Koide triples**: (e, μ, τ) and (c, b, t) each satisfy
   Koide's Q = 2/3 closure, solvable for any member from the
   other two.

They meet at **strange** and at **charm**, each of which the
framework chooses to describe as a corner building with addresses
on both roads.

---

## The core: three sharp rungs

The spine of the framework is a sequence of Apollonian closures
on the lepton seed, each sub-percent or better:

```
  Q_3  =  2/3         (e, μ, τ)                     Koide 1981
  Q_4  =  1/2         + strange at k_4 = L          algebraic identity
  Q_5  =  1/3         + charm ≈ L³                   3D Soddy, 0.02%
```

### Q_3 = 2/3 — the Koide seed

Koide's 1981 observation (Koide 1983):

    Q_3  =  (m_e + m_μ + m_τ)  /  (√m_e + √m_μ + √m_τ)²  =  2/3

holds at ppm precision with PDG values (measured Q_3 = 0.6666605
vs 2/3 = 0.6666667). Given any two of {m_e, m_μ, m_τ}, the third
is determined by a Koide quadratic. The rotations:

```
  m_τ = K₊(m_e, m_μ)   =  1776.969 MeV   (measured: 1776.86)   +0.006%
  m_e = K₋(m_μ, m_τ)   =  0.510641 MeV   (measured: 0.510999)  −0.070%
  m_μ = K₋(m_e, m_τ)   =  105.6513 MeV   (measured: 105.6584)  −0.007%
```

Koide is not a contribution of this framework. It is the **seed**
the rest is built on.

### Q_4 = 1/2 — an algebraic identity, not an empirical hit

**This step is a pure mathematical consequence of the previous
one. It contains no new physics input.** Given Q_3 = 2/3 and the
specific choice k_4 = L for the fourth circle's curvature,
Q_4 = 1/2 follows as an identity:

```
  Q_3 = 2/3 means M_3 = (2/3) S_3²
  k_4 = L = S_3 · (1 − √(2/3))
  S_4 = S_3 + L = S_3 · (2 − √(2/3))
  M_4 = M_3 + L² = S_3² · [7/3 − 2√(2/3)]
  Q_4 = M_4 / S_4² = [7/3 − 2√(2/3)] / (2 − √(2/3))² = 1/2
```

Verified numerically (Q_4 = 0.499993 with PDG lepton values).
**The Q_4 = 1/2 closure is therefore a consequence of definitions,
not a fit against data.** No claim of empirical support attaches
to this step on its own.

The **physics-content** part of this step is separate, and it is
a one-line empirical observation: **the value k_4 = L corresponds
numerically to the strange quark mass at scale Σ_lep = 1.883 GeV**.

```
  m_s(Σ_lep) measured  =  95.02 MeV       (FLAG m_s evolved to Σ_lep)
  L² represented as     =  95.12 MeV
  residual                +0.10%
```

So strange has its address at ladder position 2, and that same
value also serves as the 4th tangent circle that closes the
lepton triple into the Q_4 = 1/2 Descartes relation. The 0.10%
residual is the empirical observation. The Q_4 = 1/2 closure is
a separate (and tautological) algebraic consequence of having
chosen k_4 = L. We separate these because the reader should not
double-count them.

### Q_5 = 1/3 — the 3D Soddy closure with charm

Adding a 5th sphere at ladder position 3 (integer) lifts the
configuration to 3D. Two distinct claims appear here, and we
separate them:

**Claim A (empirical, two residuals):**

```
  s @ Σ_lep:  L²  =  95.12 MeV    vs measured 95.02 MeV    +0.10%
  c @ m_b:    L³  =  927.66 MeV   vs measured ≈ 932.4 MeV  −0.51%
```

These are the empirical observations: that strange-at-Σ_lep is
near L², and that charm-at-m_b is near L³. Sub-percent and
half-percent respectively.

**Claim B (algebraic-geometric consequence of Claim A):** *given*
those placements, the 5-sphere Soddy configuration has

```
  Q_5  =  (M_4 + L³) / (S_4 + L^(3/2))²  =  0.333406
  1/3                                       =  0.333333
  residual                                     0.022%
```

The 0.022% closeness to 1/3 is **a property of the geometry that
follows from putting strange at L² and charm at L³**, not an
independent additional fit. It is what the 3D Soddy theorem says
should hold for 5 mutually tangent 3-spheres in this
configuration (Soddy 1936). The empirical content is in the L²
and L³ placements above; Q_5 = 1/3 is the geometric consequence
of those placements being structurally tangent in the
Apollonian sense.

This separation matters because a reader could otherwise read the
"0.022% closure" as a third independent empirical hit, which it
isn't.

**Charm's primary L-ladder address is therefore integer position
3, evaluated at the m_b scale**, with the 0.51% residual we
report and live with. It is less tight than the lepton Koide or
the strange identification, but it is the natural integer slot
and it sits inside the Apollonian-tangency condition.

---

## The two road systems

### Road 1: the L-ladder

```
  position n    particle           formula                      residual
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1/3           u                  L^(1/3) + δ_QED^u            +0.07%
  2/3           d                  L^(2/3) + δ_QED^d            −2.6%   (see below)
  2             s @ Σ_lep          L²                           +0.10%
  3             c @ m_b            L³                           −0.51%
  −7            Σm_ν               L^(−7)                       (contested — see below)
```

The u and d positions are **fractional iterations** of the
integer steps, with QED self-energy corrections (δ_QED^u and
δ_QED^d). The strange and charm positions are integer. The
neutrino sum sits in the negative direction — the extension
below the lepton seed — and is discussed under open problems.

**On the QED corrections:** δ_QED^u and δ_QED^d are 1-loop
electromagnetic self-energy contributions, evaluated in the same
scheme as the FLAG light-quark inputs (MS-bar at μ = 2 GeV). The
explicit formula and its derivation, the propagation of α and
charge-ratio uncertainties, and the comparison with isospin-
breaking lattice estimates are not yet written up in this
working document; this is one of the items flagged as a
prerequisite for any external publication. The numerical
contributions used here are δ_QED^u ≈ +0.10 MeV and
δ_QED^d ≈ +0.025 MeV at μ = 2 GeV. The u-quark agreement is
strong and the d-quark agreement is the 2.6% open problem
discussed below; the framework does not invoke any *additional*
correction beyond this single 1-loop term to close either gap.

### Road 2: the Koide triples

```
  triple                   Q       residual         evaluation scales
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  (e, μ, τ)                2/3     0.0006%          all at rest mass
  (c, b, t)                2/3     ≈ 0.001%         m_c at μ = m_τ,
                                                    m_b and m_t at self
```

The lepton triple is Koide 1981. The heavy triple is the new
structural observation in this work, in the spirit of (and
revising) the heavy-quark extensions attempted by Rivero (2011)
and others. Given any two members, the third is solvable via
Koide rotation:

```
  m_t from K₊(m_c(m_τ), m_b)  =  162.425 GeV   vs 162.5 GeV   −0.046%
  m_b from K₋(m_c(m_τ), m_t)  =  4.206 GeV     vs 4.203 GeV   +0.074%
  m_c from K₋(m_b, m_t)        =  1.144 GeV    vs 1.143 GeV   +0.126%
```

All three sit well inside current measurement uncertainty on the
heavy-quark inputs.

**The crucial structural input is that charm is evaluated at the
m_τ scale**, not at its self-scale. We want to be very clear about
what this is and isn't. We did not search across scales for one
that closes the relation and then report the closing one; the
m_τ choice has a structural story (m_τ is the heaviest lepton,
sitting just below the charm hadronization threshold at m_D, and
is the natural lepton-side endpoint of the L-ladder before the
hadronic continuum begins). However, *that story is not yet a
derivation*. We do not have an a-priori principle from which
"evaluate m_c at μ = m_τ" falls out. Until that principle is
found, the heavy Koide closure is best read **not as a confirmed
empirical result, but as a numerical puzzle**: there exists a
specific scale at which the (c, b, t) triple closes to Koide
Q = 2/3, that scale is the heaviest lepton mass, and we don't
know why. **Why m_τ specifically is the single most important
open theoretical question in the framework.** It is flagged as a
prerequisite for any external publication of this work (see
below).

For completeness: we did check neighboring evaluation scales for
charm. Self-scale (m_c) closes the triple to roughly 0.7% — about
two orders of magnitude looser than the m_τ evaluation. The m_b
scale closes to roughly 0.5%. The m_τ scale closes to roughly
0.001%. The closeness at m_τ is what makes the puzzle sharp; it
is also what makes the methodological caution above necessary.

### Where the two roads meet

Two particles end up with addresses on both road systems
simultaneously. Following the multiply-connected-address concept
introduced earlier, we choose to describe these as **corner
buildings**: same particle, two addresses, one per road.

**Strange** sits at L² on the L-ladder AND is the 4th tangent
circle that closes the lepton Koide triple into Q_4 = 1/2. As
discussed under the Q_4 section above, these are not two
independent observations — the Q_4 closure is the algebraic
consequence of the L² placement.

**Charm** sits at L³ on the L-ladder (at scale m_b) AND is a
vertex of the heavy Koide triple (at scale m_τ). The framework
chooses to describe charm as a corner building with two
addresses, one on each road. We do not insist on a global
geometric embedding in which both addresses sit cleanly on a
flat map; what we have is local validity at each address. The
same particle can be reached from either street, and on either
street the local description is correct — modulo the open
question above about why the m_τ scale is the right one for the
heavy-Koide road.

---

## Complete particle map (mass order)

Rows are ordered by the measured mass of each particle, low to
high, rather than by SM discovery order. **This is how the road
structure becomes visible**: mass-order the actual measured
values, and the L-ladder positions and Koide-triple membership
fall into place. Charm appears on **two rows** because the
framework chooses to describe it as a corner building, with one
address on each of the two road systems it participates in.

```
particle   scale        measured          framework address          represented as   residual
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
e          rest         0.5109989 MeV     K₋(m_μ, m_τ) or input      0.510641 MeV     −0.070%
u          2 GeV        2.16 ± 0.07 MeV   L^(1/3) + δ_QED^u           2.1614 MeV       +0.07%
d          2 GeV        4.70 ± 0.05 MeV   L^(2/3) + δ_QED^d           4.5767 MeV       −2.6%   ★
s          Σ_lep        95.02 MeV         L²                          95.117 MeV       +0.10%
μ          rest         105.6584 MeV      K₋(m_e, m_τ) or input       105.6513 MeV     −0.007%
c          m_b          ≈ 932.4 MeV       L³                          927.66 MeV       −0.51%
c          m_τ          1142.62 MeV       K₋(m_b, m_t)  ← corner      1144.07 MeV      +0.126%
τ          rest         1776.86 MeV       K₊(m_e, m_μ)                1776.969 MeV     +0.006%
b          m_b          4203 ± 11 MeV     K₋(m_c(m_τ), m_t)           4206.1 MeV       +0.074%
t          m_t          162.5 ± 0.7 GeV   K₊(m_c(m_τ), m_b)           162.425 GeV      −0.046%

Σmν        cosmological < 0.072 eV (DESI) L^(−7)                      0.1192 eV        in tension ★
```

★ = open problem, discussed below.

The framework uses 5 explicit numerical inputs (m_e, m_μ, α_s,
m_c(m_c), and m_b or m_t) and represents 8 other charged-fermion
masses to sub-percent residuals using algebraic relations already
in the literature, plus 1 loose (d) and 1 contested (Σmν). For
the structural choices that sit alongside the explicit inputs,
see the parameter-count discussion above.

"Represented as" rather than "predicted as" is deliberate
throughout. The framework does not predict any of these masses —
they are all measured. What it does is **place each measured
value at a structural position in a two-road system**, and the
residual column reports how close the placement is. This is
compression of the description, not new physics.

---

## Dimensional content: ratios, not absolutes

The framework's content is a system of mass **ratios**. For any
two particles X and Y with ladder positions n_X and n_Y:

    m_X / m_Y  =  L^(n_X − n_Y)

Both sides are pure numbers regardless of how L's units are
interpreted. To get absolute masses, anchor one (m_e in MeV) and
read the rest off as ratios. This is the same structure as
standard QCD: α(μ) is dimensionless, m_q / m_q' is dimensionless,
and one absolute mass (m_p or m_e) fixes the GeV scale.

Verified ratios (sub-0.5%):

```
  c(m_b) / s              =  L^1          9.753          (direct integer step)
  Σmν / s                 =  L^(−9)       1.253e−9       (if L^(−7) holds)
  s / u                   =  L^(5/3)      44.52          (with u from L^(1/3) + δ_QED)
  c(self) / c(m_b)        =  L^(π−3)      1.381          (the QCD running factor)
```

The last row is the QCD running factor for charm between the m_b
and self scales, expressed as a power of L. The exponent
**π − 3 ≈ 0.14159** is discussed under speculations.

Reading the framework as ratios instead of absolutes resolves
what looked like a dimensional ambiguity in earlier drafts. L
raised to any exponent is a pure number; multiplied by an anchor
mass, it becomes an absolute mass. The framework never asked
more than one anchor.

## Open problems

### Down quark: 2.6% residual and honestly flagged

```
  L^(2/3) + δ_QED^d at 2 GeV  =  4.577 MeV
  m_d(2 GeV) measured          =  4.70 ± 0.05 MeV    (FLAG)
  deviation                       −2.6%   (≈ 2.5 measurement σ on the lattice value)
```

The down quark is the only non-sub-percent residual in the
charged fermion sector. The 1-loop QED self-energy correction is
applied (the same formula that closes m_u to within measurement
uncertainty), but QED for d is ~4× smaller than for u (charge
ratio squared: (1/3)² vs (2/3)²), and it doesn't close the gap.

We applied **no other corrections** and we deliberately do
**not** fit the scale. The relation becomes exact at μ*_d ≈ 2.228
GeV, but this energy corresponds to no known physical scale in
the framework or in QCD, and the framework did not search for a
scale that would force the hit. The 2.6% residual is what it is
at μ = 2 GeV, the standard FLAG reference point.

The pion-spacing observation `μ*_d − μ*_u ≈ 139.6 MeV ≈ m_π±` to
0.05% is a structural relation between the u and d natural scales
that does track cleanly, but it does not anchor either scale
absolutely.

**Three possible fates:**

1. A reformulation of the L^(2/3) position for d, perhaps
   involving a different structure at ladder position 2/3.
2. A lattice move — the FLAG value could shift inside the
   framework's predicted band as precision improves. There is
   precedent for lattice values shifting at this magnitude
   between FLAG editions.
3. **The framework is wrong in the light quark sector and this
   single hit falsifies that piece.** This is a real possibility
   and we are not absorbing the residual to avoid it.

We flag it and leave it. No correction invented to force the fit.

### Neutrinos: L^(−7) is likely incomplete or incorrect

```
  L^(−7)                   =  0.1192 eV    (framework value)
  DESI Y1 + Planck bound   <  0.072 eV    (in tension)
  Planck + BAO (older)     <  0.12 eV     (marginal)
```

No QED correction applies to neutrinos (Q = 0 exactly). The
1-loop weak analog (Z self-energy) is about 5.6 meV total across
Σmν, closing only ~12% of the gap to DESI. The L^(−7) value
stands or falls on its own.

**Required structural commitments if L^(−7) is correct:**

- Inverted neutrino mass hierarchy (Σmν must be above 0.099 eV).
- The DESI bound must relax, not tighten, as more data comes in.

**Status:** drifting away from viability. We disclose this
honestly. The framework likely needs revision or reformulation in
the neutrino sector, **and a clean failure here is one of the
ways this piece of the framework gets killed**. A failure here
does **not** propagate upward — the charged sector remains intact
regardless of what happens with the neutrino extension — but the
absence of upward propagation is itself a claim that an external
version of this framework would need to defend, not just assert.

**Falsification timeline:**

- DESI Year 3 (2026): Σmν bound tightens to ~0.05 eV or detection.
- DESI Year 5 (2028): ~0.04 eV bound or detection.
- JUNO (2027–2030): neutrino hierarchy resolved at 3–4σ.

Within 4 years this piece will either survive or die.

## Prerequisites for external publication

This is a working document, not a journal paper. Before any
version of this framework is appropriate for external submission,
several methodological obligations have to be discharged. Listing
them here so the working doc and any future paper register stay
honest about the gap.

1. **Why m_τ for charm in the heavy Koide triple.** The single
   most important open theoretical question. A structural
   derivation, or at minimum a principled story for which scale
   ought to be used and why m_τ is it. Without this, the heavy
   Koide closure is a numerical puzzle, not a result.

2. **A common-scale or scheme-invariant restatement.** The
   framework currently mixes evaluation scales across particles
   (u, d at 2 GeV; s at Σ_lep; c at m_b and m_τ; b at m_b; t at
   m_t). The L-ladder claim should be restated either at one
   common scale, or in a manifestly RG-invariant form, with a
   sensitivity check showing the structure does not appear or
   disappear under reasonable alternative conventions.

3. **An explicit accounting of structural choices.** Beyond the
   five numerical inputs, the framework makes structural choices
   (particle-to-relation assignment, scale per particle, Koide
   rotation root selection). A publishable version has to
   enumerate these and discuss whether they should count toward
   the parameter budget.

4. **Full uncertainty propagation.** The reported residuals are
   at the 0.1–0.5% level for the sharpest claims. Theoretical
   uncertainties from α_s, threshold matching, scheme conversion,
   and correlated lattice inputs need to be propagated through
   CRunDec and added to the residual budget. It is currently
   possible that some of the sub-percent residuals are
   comparable in size to omitted theoretical systematics.

5. **A null-model / coincidence-frequency control.** For the
   speculative observations (π in charm running, top near the
   unit-ball volume peak, pion spacing), and arguably for the
   sub-percent residuals more generally, there should be an
   explicit estimate of how often coincidences of similar
   tightness would arise under a generic null model
   (random label permutations, generic low-parameter fits over
   the same input set). Without this, the reader cannot calibrate
   how surprising the observed residuals are.

6. **Explicit δ_QED formula and propagation.** The 1-loop QED
   self-energy contributions used for u and d need to be written
   out with the formula, the scheme, the input values, and the
   propagated uncertainty.

7. **Verification of citation arXiv numbers.** Several
   references in this document are from memory and need to be
   verified against current arXiv search before being cited
   externally.

None of these change the structural content of the framework.
All of them change what level of confidence the structural
content can be presented at, and what an external reader is
entitled to demand.

## Speculations

These are not claims. They are observations we noticed and
haven't explained.

### Why does π appear in the charm running?

The integer claim is **L³ = m_c at μ = m_b**, at 0.51% residual.
Running to self-scale via QCD gives a factor of ~1.374 at 4-loop
(and ~1.379 at the all-orders extrapolation). The framework's
ratio reading gives:

    m_c(m_c) / m_c(m_b)  =  L^(π − 3)  =  1.3806

which matches the all-orders extrapolated QCD running factor to
0.1% and the 4-loop value to 0.5%. The small exponent
**π − 3 ≈ 0.14159** lives in the running, not in the integer
position.

What makes this interesting: at leading-order QCD (where the
mass anomalous dimension has no π's at all), the running ratio
is ~1.215, way off from L^(π−3). At NLO, NNLO, N³LO, the ratio
converges monotonically toward L^(π−3) as higher-loop integrals
contribute π^n terms via ζ(2) = π²/6, ζ(3), etc. This was the
loop-order convergence result from the 2026-04-12 session.

So π "sneaks in" through higher-loop QCD corrections and the
series happens to converge to L^(π−3). Why this specific value?
Unknown. It could be:

- A **coincidence** — the series happens to land near L raised
  to a small π-containing number and we're pattern-matching.
- A **genuine identity** — there is a reason the QCD running for
  charm between the m_b and m_c scales equals L^(π−3) exactly,
  and we haven't found it.
- A **rotation artifact** — if charm is a common frame between
  the L-ladder and the heavy Koide triple, there may be a
  rotation structure whose projection produces π.

**Explicit caveat:** the claim "π appears here and therefore
there is a rotation with charm as the pivot" is overclaiming at
current precision. The numerical overlap is real; the geometric
interpretation is speculation. A null-model coincidence-frequency
estimate (see prerequisites above) would tell us how surprised
we should actually be by this overlap.

### Three generations as structural fit

The L-ladder and the Koide triples together accommodate exactly
the SM charged fermions:

```
  Lepton Koide triple:     e, μ, τ          (the seed)
  Fractional L-positions:  u at L^(1/3),
                           d at L^(2/3)     (below the seed)
  Integer L-positions:     s at L²,
                           c at L³          (above the seed)
  Heavy Koide triple:      c, b, t          (charm as corner)
```

All 9 charged fermions are placed. There is no residual particle.
Adding a fourth generation would require either a new Koide
triple with its own Q = 2/3 closure (no observed one) or a new
ladder position not already filled (none exist at the integer or
simple fractional slots).

In this reading, three generations is not a derivation but a
clean structural fit: the SM has exactly the right number of
charged fermions to occupy the framework's positions without
overflow or remainder.

**Caveat:** this is a descriptive observation, not a proof. The
framework doesn't independently predict "three" — it describes a
set of structural slots, and the SM happens to fill them.

### Heavy quarks, confinement, and resolution

The L-ladder's fundamental-accessible positions end at charm
(~1.3 GeV). The next slot upward is at ~1 GeV, where the SM has
no fundamental particle. A reading consistent with this is that
**heavy quarks (b, t) cannot sit on the low-mass ladder as
fundamentals and must confine** — they have no fundamental slot
at the right scale. The proton is numerically close to an
extended slot at Q_6 = 1/4 (residual ≈ 0.021%), but the slot is a
broad valley admitting any ~1 GeV hadron, so this is not a sharp
particle-by-particle prediction.

The address-book metaphor is useful here. Japanese addresses are
hierarchical: country → prefecture → city → ward → block →
building. Different sectors of the framework operate at different
levels of this drill-down.

For the charged fundamental fermions, the framework reaches
**building-level resolution**: e, μ, τ, s, c each get a specific
address with sub-percent residuals. We can name the building.

For the hadron-mass region around 1 GeV, the framework reaches
only **prefecture-level resolution**. What the Q_6 = 1/4
observation tells you is that there is a "prefecture" near 1 GeV
where the SM packs a dense cloud of hadronic states (the baryon
octet, the η', the scalar mesons, the φ, K*, etc.). The
framework can address that prefecture — "around 1 GeV, on the
road past charm" — but it does not resolve down to which building
within the prefecture. The proton sits in the right prefecture;
so does the neutron; so do half a dozen other hadrons. We don't
have street-level resolution there.

This isn't a failure. It's a statement about what resolution the
framework operates at for different sectors. For the charged
fundamental fermions, building-level. For the hadronic continuum,
prefecture-level. Both are valid uses of the same address book;
they operate at different layers of the hierarchy.

The heavy Koide triple (c, b, t) is the heavy quarks' separate
structural home. Charm acts as the corner between the low-mass
ladder and the heavy triple — same particle, two addresses, two
roads.

**Caveat:** this is interpretation of the existing structure, not
an independent prediction. The qualitative picture is consistent
with the ladder; the specific claim "the proton uniquely fills
the Q_6 = 1/4 slot" is not supported by the statistics (the slot
is a broad valley).

### Top quark near the unit-ball volume peak

The volume of the n-dimensional unit ball is

    V_n  =  π^(n/2) / Γ(n/2 + 1)

which peaks, as a function of n treated as a continuous variable,
at

    n*  =  5.2569464...        (solution of ψ(n/2 + 1) = ln π)

with peak value V(n*) ≈ 5.2778. Past this dimension the volume
of the unit ball actually *decreases*. This is a standard fact
from high-dimensional geometry.

**Top sits near this peak dimension on the L-ladder:**

    log_L(m_t)  =  log_L(162.5 GeV)  =  5.2681
    n*           =                      5.2569
    difference   =                      +0.0112   (+0.21% in the exponent)

In the exponent this is a 0.21% match. In mass terms, if we
demanded top sit at n* exactly we would represent it at L^n* =
158.4 GeV, vs measured 162.5 ± 0.7 GeV — a 4.1 GeV miss, well
outside current measurement uncertainty on m_t. So this is
**not** a framework-precision hit in the strict sense.

However, because the volume function has a true maximum at n*
with dV/dn = 0 there, the volume at log_L(m_t) is essentially at
the peak:

    V(5.2681) / V(5.2569)  =  99.9995%

This says top sits at 99.9995% of the maximum unit-ball volume
across dimensions — but this is largely a flat-maximum artifact
(the peak is so flat that many nearby positions round to ~100%
of V_peak). The honest reading: top is a **near-miss** of the
dimensional peak, not a sharp hit.

Status: **suggestive coincidence, not a prediction.** Flagged
because:

1. Top is the unique SM fundamental near log_L ≈ 5.26 — no other
   particle is in that neighborhood. Bottom is at 3.66, next
   below.
2. Top is already independently represented by the heavy Koide
   rotation at the 0.05% level, so the unit-ball observation is
   an independent question about *where* the heavy-Koide-placed
   top happens to land on the L-ladder — and it lands near the
   dimensional peak of the unit ball.
3. The 2.6% mass gap (or equivalently 0.21% exponent gap) sits
   outside framework precision and is reported as such.

If future precision on m_t or on L moves the log_L(m_t) position
onto n* exactly, this would upgrade from coincidence to claim.
Until then it is a near-miss worth recording.

## What this is and isn't

**It IS:**

- A representation. A single coherent map of the SM charged
  fermion mass sector, organized so that relationships between
  particles become visible.
- Built on math already in the literature: Koide (1983),
  Soddy (1936), Kocik (2007, 2012), 4-loop QCD running
  (CRunDec lineage), FLAG lattice averages, and existing
  heavy-Koide attempts (Rivero 2011 and others). No new
  mathematics introduced.
- Sub-percent structural residuals for 8 of 12 fermion masses
  from 5 explicit numerical inputs plus a small set of
  enumerated structural choices.
- Internally consistent as a structural reading, with two
  stubborn exceptions (down quark, neutrino sum) honestly flagged
  and not absorbed into the picture.

**It is NOT:**

- A theory. It does not give a Lagrangian, a symmetry group, or
  a mechanism for generating masses.
- A prediction of new particles. It places known ones at
  structural positions.
- A claim that the Standard Model is wrong. The SM is a valid
  Japanese address book. This is a Western address book for the
  same territory. Both can be correct simultaneously.
- A statistically significant empirical result. The residuals
  are real, but residual-vs-error ratios in an exploratory
  representational construction are not p-values, and the
  framework does not yet have the null-model control that would
  let a reader calibrate how surprising the residuals actually
  are.
- The unique geometric projection. Other projections of the same
  Koide algebraic constraint may also work. The opposite of a
  true idea may also be true — we genuinely don't know if this
  is the right way to read the SM mass sector, we only know the
  math works at sub-percent for most of the charged fermions.
- A reduction of free parameters of any underlying theory. It is
  compression of the existing description, using only measured
  inputs plus a small set of enumerated structural choices.
- A resolution of the down-quark 2.6% miss or the neutrino
  sector tension. Both are open problems flagged with honest
  disclosure and falsification timelines.
- Self-insulating by stance. The "representation, not theory"
  framing is not an attempt to evade methodological scrutiny —
  it is an attempt to match the precision of the claim to the
  precision of what is actually being asserted. The
  prerequisites-for-publication list above is the part of the
  scrutiny we accept and intend to discharge before any external
  version.

---

## Falsification timeline

Every part of this framework is testable on convergence in
future measurements. This is not special to the neutrino sector.

```
  year       experiment         what it would affect
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ongoing    FLAG lattice       tighter m_s, m_c, m_b, m_d updates
  2026       DESI Y3            Σmν bound → kills or preserves L^(−7)
  2026       m_c(m_c) updates   tightens the 0.5% residual at charm
  2027–30    JUNO               neutrino hierarchy resolved
  2028       DESI Y5            Σmν near-detection or tight bound
  2030+      CMB-S4             Σmν detection if Σmν ≈ 0.1 eV
  2040+      FCC-ee             m_t to ±0.05 GeV (10× heavy Koide tightening)
```

The L^(−7) neutrino piece will be tested or killed within 4
years. The down-quark 2.6% residual will either settle with
lattice refinements or remain a structural wound. Everything
else (the lepton Koide, the L-ladder for u, s, c, and the heavy
Koide closure conditional on the m_τ-scale puzzle being
explained) is supported at current precision and will tighten
as measurements improve.

---

## In one paragraph

Standard particle physics labels the twelve fermion masses as
twelve independent numbers, in the order the particles were
discovered. This framework proposes a dual description in which
the same masses are positions on two intersecting roads — an
L-power ladder for u, d, s, c and Koide Q = 2/3 triples for the
charged leptons (e, μ, τ) and the heavy quarks (c, b, t). The
two roads meet at strange and charm. Charm is described as a
corner building with two addresses: L³ on the L-ladder at scale
m_b, and a vertex of the heavy Koide triple at scale m_τ. Five
explicit numerical inputs plus a small set of enumerated
structural choices represent eight other charged-fermion masses
to sub-percent residuals using algebraic relations already in
the literature (Koide 1983, Soddy 1936, Kocik 2007/2012, plus
4-loop QCD running and FLAG lattice averages). The underlying
spine is the Apollonian sphere-packing sequence Q_3 = 2/3 →
Q_4 = 1/2 → Q_5 = 1/3, where Q_3 is Koide, Q_4 is an algebraic
identity that follows from the L² placement of strange, and
Q_5 is the geometric consequence of having put both strange at
L² and charm at L³. The down quark is a 2.6% open problem at
the standard 2 GeV scale, flagged honestly without a fitted
correction. The neutrino sum extension Σmν = L^(−7) ≈ 0.12 eV
is in tension with DESI Y1, likely incomplete or incorrect in
that sector, and does not propagate upward. The heavy Koide
triple closes to roughly 0.001% only when charm is evaluated at
the m_τ scale; *why* that is the right scale is the single most
important open theoretical question and a prerequisite for any
external publication. The sharp claim is **a representation, not
a theory**: the structural placements are tight, the parameter
accounting is small once structural choices are enumerated, and
the map is offered in the spirit of the periodic table —
earning its place by making relationships visible, not by
deriving them from first principles.

## References

The framework is built entirely on results already in the
literature. Citations below are grouped by topic. arXiv numbers
are given where the author is confident; older works (Koide
1983, Soddy 1936) predate arXiv and use journal references.

### Koide formula and prior lepton-sector work

- **Koide, Y.** (1982). "A fermion-boson composite model of
  quarks and leptons." Lett. Nuovo Cim. 34, 201. — first
  appearance of the formula.
- **Koide, Y.** (1983). "A fermion-boson composite model of
  quarks and leptons." Phys. Lett. B 120, 161. — the standard
  citation; the relation Q = 2/3 for the charged leptons.
- **Rivero, A., & Gsponer, A.** (2005). "The strange formula of
  Dr. Koide." A review and reframing of Koide's relation that
  brought the formula to broader attention. Available on arXiv.
- **Brannen, C. A.** (2006 onward). "The lepton masses." A
  circulant-matrix representation of Koide's formula that
  exposes the lepton phase 2/9. See Brannen's website and
  subsequent arXiv notes.
- **Rivero, A.** (2011). "A new Koide tuple: strange-charm-
  bottom." arXiv:1111.7232. — early attempt at a heavy-quark
  Koide closure; this framework's heavy triple (c, b, t) is in
  the same lineage but uses a different scale convention for
  charm.

### Apollonian / circle-packing geometry

- **Soddy, F.** (1936). "The kiss precise." Nature 137, 1021.
  — the Descartes circle theorem in 2D and its 3D Soddy
  generalization, in poetic form.
- **Kocik, J.** (2007). "A theorem on circle configurations."
  arXiv:0706.0372. — generalized Descartes theorem for circles
  intersecting at non-zero angles. The mathematical foundation
  for reading Koide's relation as a circle configuration.
- **Kocik, J.** (2012). "The Koide lepton mass formula and
  geometry of circles." arXiv:1201.2067. — explicit reading of
  Koide as a 2D configuration of three circles intersecting a
  fourth (degenerate) line at angle arccos(1/3) ≈ 70.53°.
- **Kocik, J.** (2019). arXiv:1910.09174. — generalized Descartes
  configurations and tangency spinors.
- **Kocik, J.** (2020). arXiv:2001.05866. — integer Apollonian
  packings and their spinor classification.

The framework's Apollonian / Soddy reading follows Kocik's
geometric formulation. As discussed above, this is one of
several possible geometric projections of the underlying Koide
algebraic constraint.

### QCD running and lattice values

- **Chetyrkin, K. G., Kühn, J. H., & Steinhauser, M.** (2000).
  "RunDec: A Mathematica package for running and decoupling of
  the strong coupling and quark masses." Comput. Phys. Commun.
  133, 43. arXiv:hep-ph/0004189. — the engine for 4-loop QCD
  mass running. CRunDec is the C++ reimplementation used for
  numerical work in this document; subsequent updates
  (Schmidt, Steinhauser, et al.) extend to 5 loops where
  available.
- **FLAG Collaboration** ("Flavour Lattice Averaging Group"),
  most recent review (2024 edition at time of writing). World
  averages of light and heavy quark masses (m_u, m_d, m_s, m_c,
  m_b) from lattice QCD. The framework uses FLAG values
  throughout for measured-quark-mass comparisons.

### Cosmological neutrino bounds

- **DESI Collaboration** (Adame et al.) (2024). "DESI 2024 VI:
  Cosmological constraints from the measurements of baryon
  acoustic oscillations." arXiv:2404.03002. — current tightest
  cosmological upper bound on Σmν (< 0.072 eV combined with
  Planck).
- **Planck Collaboration** (2018). "Planck 2018 results. VI.
  Cosmological parameters." arXiv:1807.06209. — older Planck +
  BAO bound (< 0.12 eV), marginally consistent with the
  framework's L^(−7) ≈ 0.119 eV value.

### Methodological / inspirational

- **Mendeleev, D.** (1869). The original periodic table of the
  elements. Cited in spirit, as the canonical example of a
  representation that earns its place by making relationships
  visible without deriving them from first principles.
- **Sivers, D.** (2009). "Weird, or just different?" TED Talks.
  https://www.ted.com/talks/derek_sivers_weird_or_just_different
  — independent parallel articulation of the address-system
  observation. The author has lived in Japan since 2010 and the
  observation in this document is independent; Sivers is cited
  as a parallel articulation, not as a source.

## The Japan address problem

Japanese addresses don't name streets. They name blocks. The roads
between blocks are the unnamed spaces. To find a building in Japan
you walk down a hierarchical drill-down: country, then prefecture,
then city, then ward, then block, then a house number. House
numbers within a block aren't in spatial order; they're assigned
in the order the houses were built. From outside the system this
looks random. From inside it is perfectly complete: every building
has an address, and every address locates exactly one building.

The author has lived in Japan since 2010 — over fifteen years at
time of writing — and the observation that this is the inverse of
the Western system has been sitting around for a while. (Derek
Sivers makes essentially the same observation in his 2009 TED talk
*Weird, or just different?* as a parallel articulation. His
closing line is the one that matters: whatever idea you hold, the
opposite may also be true.)

The Standard Model's list of fermion masses is a Japanese address
book in two ways at once:

1. **It names the buildings, not the roads.** Each particle
   carries its own value (m_e, m_μ, m_τ, m_u, m_d, m_s, m_c, m_b,
   m_t, plus three neutrino masses). The relationships between
   these values — the structural roads running between them — are
   not part of the standard description. You memorize the
   buildings.

2. **The numbering is construction order, not structure.** The
   quark labels u, d, s, c, b, t come from discovery order. Up
   and down from the 1964 quark-parton proposal. Strange from
   the "strange particles" of the 1950s. Charm from the November
   Revolution of 1974. Bottom in 1977, top in 1995. "Generation
   1, 2, 3" is assigned chronologically by when particles were
   first observed, not by any logical criterion tied to their
   properties. These are construction-order house numbers,
   exactly the Japanese pattern.

Both naming systems are complete. They name opposite things.

## What this is: a representation

This document proposes a dual description of the same particles —
one that names the roads where the SM names the buildings. **It is
not a theory. It is not a set of predictions. It is not a claim
that the Standard Model is wrong.** It is a representation: a
human-readable rendering of the same underlying physics, organized
so that relationships between particles become visible at a
glance.

The closest analogy is the periodic table. Mendeleev didn't derive
carbon from first principles. He placed it in a grid where its
relationships to neighbors became obvious — why carbon behaves
like silicon, why lithium behaves like sodium, why helium and neon
are inert. The grid itself isn't physical. It's a convention for
drawing the same chemistry in a way that makes patterns navigable.
The SM particle chart isn't physical either. Both are
representations that earn their place by making the underlying
relationships visible without replacing the underlying math.

The framework inverts the usual direction. The standard direction
is: start with the measured fermion masses, look for patterns
among them. That has been done many times — by Koide (1981, 1983)
with the original lepton formula, by Rivero & Gsponer (2005) in
revisiting and reframing it, by Brannen (2006 onward) with the
circulant-matrix representation that exposes the lepton phase
2/9, and by Rivero (2011) and others in attempting heavy-quark
extensions. **This framework runs the same direction backwards.**
It starts with lepton curvature L as a coordinate axis and lets
each particle find a position on that axis. Mass ratios fall out
as differences in position. RG running becomes a correction *from*
a base position — like a street address that acquires a minor
refinement as you approach the actual building.

**The one rule the framework asks you to accept is this: lepton
energies define a position.** Given the three measured lepton
masses, compute L, and use L as a coordinate. Everything else
follows from this one choice plus mathematics already in the
literature: Koide's algebraic relation (Koide 1983); Soddy's
circle theorem in 2D and 3D (Soddy 1936); Kocik's generalized
Descartes theorem and his explicit reading of Koide as a circle
configuration (Kocik 2007, 2012, 2019, 2020); 4-loop QCD
anomalous-dimension running for quark masses (Chetyrkin, Kühn,
Steinhauser, the RunDec / CRunDec lineage from 2000 onward); and
the current lattice averages for quark masses from the FLAG
collaboration. Full references at the end.

The geometric interpretation we use is Apollonian / Soddy circle
packing, following Kocik. **It is not the only possible geometric
projection of the same algebraic content.** The Koide identity is
an algebraic constraint, and any geometry that realizes that
constraint is a candidate projection. Other geometric readings
(linked tori, gasket fractals, spinor configurations) may also
work. We choose this projection because it organizes the data
cleanly and connects to existing literature; we are not claiming
it is the unique one. Geometry is a projection of algebra.

Address systems are tools. Japanese addresses are good for finding
a specific building when you know the prefecture; Western
addresses are good for seeing which buildings sit next to each
other on a street. Neither is "the real geography" — both are
projections that make different things visible, and you can mix
and match freely depending on which question you're asking. The
framework's choice is to **reorder** the SM fermion masses by
magnitude, then ask what connects them, and to use Koide's
relation as the connector because it was already there in the
literature, it is accurate, and it is geometric in a way that
lets you picture and navigate the structure. Reorder, readdress,
look at how the entities are actually connected. **The geometry
simply follows.**

### A note on language: residuals, not significances

Throughout this document we report deviations as **percent
residuals** ("0.10% deviation," "0.51% residual") rather than as
multiples of measurement error ("σ"). This is deliberate. The
framework is an exploratory representational construction over a
high-dimensional space of choices (which particles, which
groupings, which scales, which exponents); residual-vs-error
ratios are not valid p-values in that setting, because the
implicit number of trials is not controlled and there is no
prespecified search protocol. The percent residuals report what
the framework actually says: how close a structural placement
sits to a measured value. Whether that closeness is *evidentially*
meaningful, against a null model of generic numerological
coincidence, is a separate question that this document does not
answer. (See "Prerequisites for external publication" below.)

### What this is doing for the parameter count

If you accept the one rule — that lepton energies can define a
position — the same twelve charged-fermion masses can be described
with a smaller set of explicit inputs: m_e, m_μ, α_s(M_Z),
m_c(m_c), and one heavy-quark anchor (m_b or m_t). The other
masses sit at structural positions in the map and follow from
those inputs plus the math listed above.

**This is compression of the description, not reduction of free
parameters in an underlying theory.** The Standard Model's
parameter count remains what it is. What we have done is
reorganize the same information so that relationships between
particles are visible — without invoking new physics, new forces,
fitted scales, or hidden parameters. The reorganization uses
**measured and RG-evolved masses** (FLAG lattice values, evolved
between scales via 4-loop CRunDec where needed); we
**mass-order them on the map, and the road structure becomes
visible**. Whether this reorganization eventually constrains
flavor-model building, or serves only as a useful navigational
map on its own, we do not claim. We are offering the map.

A note on what "compression" honestly counts: the explicit
numerical inputs are five, but the framework also makes structural
choices (which particles belong on the L-ladder vs. on a Koide
triple; which scale each mass is evaluated at; which Koide
rotation root is taken). These structural choices are not "free
parameters" in the usual sense — they are made once, before
looking at residuals, and are then fixed — but they are also not
nothing. A fully honest parameter accounting for an external
paper would have to enumerate them. This working document does
not yet do so.

### The epistemic stance that follows

- **Is the math already there?** Yes. Koide (1981, 1983), Rivero
  & Gsponer (2005), Brannen (2006), Rivero (2011), Soddy (1936),
  Kocik (2007, 2012, 2019, 2020), the CRunDec lineage, and the
  FLAG lattice averages are all already published. The framework
  arranges these into a single coherent map; it does not
  introduce new mathematics.
- **Does it represent the SM charged fermion masses accurately?**
  Yes — to sub-percent residuals for 8 of the 12 fermion masses.
  Two (the down quark and the neutrino sum) are honestly flagged
  as stubborn.
- **Does it predict new particles?** No. It places the known ones.
- **Is it the correct answer?** Unknown, and "correct" may not
  even be the right question to ask of a representation. Other
  maps may also be valid.
- **Is there math underlying the geometry?** Yes. Geometry is a
  projection of algebra. We use the Apollonian / Soddy projection
  because it organizes the data cleanly; other projections may
  also work.

### A methodological note

This framework is what one sees when approaching the SM mass
sector by **mass-ordering first and labeling second**, rather
than the other way around. The standard pedagogical path teaches
u, d, s, c, b, t and the three generations as primary and the
actual numerical values as secondary metadata — by the time a
physicist is fluent enough to look for structure among the
masses, the discovery-order labels feel natural, the magnitudes
feel like attached annotations, and the question "what do these
numbers actually look like in order" rarely gets asked.

Reversing that order — treating the measured masses as the
primary objects and the labels as construction-order house
numbers attached after the fact — is what makes the road
structure visible. We mention this not as autobiography but as a
note on what the framework is and isn't: it is a structural map
produced by approaching the mass sector with the labels
deliberately demoted, and it may be harder to see if you come at
it with the labels already cemented as primary.

## Multiply-connected addresses

Before getting to the math, one feature of the address metaphor
matters for what comes later. In any address system, some
buildings have more than one address. A house on a corner faces
two streets and gets a number on each. A building with a back
door reachable from a different street is findable from both
directions. Both addresses are correct; the address you use
depends on which side you're walking in from.

When we organize the SM charged fermions into the framework's two
road systems, **some particles turn out to be multiply addressed
in this sense**. The L-ladder gives one road, the heavy Koide
triple gives another, and any particle that participates in both
ends up with one address on each.

A consequence of this: the global geometry doesn't have to embed
cleanly in any low number of dimensions. We are not claiming the
road system can be drawn on a single sheet of paper without
overlap, or that the underlying connectivity is geometrically
simple. We are claiming that the **local description** at each
particle works — that you can stand at the position of the tau
lepton and look one street over to the lepton-sum scale, and the
address structure makes sense locally. As more connections are
eventually found, the global picture may need higher dimensions,
or may become physically impossible to embed in any flat
geometry. **That is allowed.** The purpose of an address book is
local navigation, not a globally embeddable map.

The geometric shape improves understanding of the local
neighborhood around each particle. It does not have to be
extendable to a single coherent global picture, and it does not
have to commit to a particular dimensionality of the embedding
space. The framework's claim is local, not global.

This matters for charm in particular. Charm will end up
multiply-addressed in the road system below — once on the
L-ladder and once as a vertex of the heavy Koide triple — and the
framework chooses to describe this as a corner-building pattern.
Both addresses sit at framework-level closeness; neither is
"more real" than the other.

## The framework in one paragraph

Define the **lepton seed**

    L  =  √m_e + √m_μ + √m_τ  −  √(m_e + m_μ + m_τ)  ≈  9.7528

This is the outer Soddy curvature of the lepton triple in the
Apollonian-circle reading of Koide's formula (Kocik 2012). Each
particle is assigned a **ladder position** n. For any two
particles X and Y, their mass ratio is

    m_X / m_Y  =  L^(n_X − n_Y)

Both sides of this equation are pure numbers regardless of how
L's units are interpreted. The framework represents particles as
dimensionless ratios; one external anchor (typically m_e in MeV)
fixes the absolute scale.

There are **two road systems**:

1. The **L-ladder**: u, d, s, c each sit at a power of L.
2. The **Koide triples**: (e, μ, τ) and (c, b, t) each satisfy
   Koide's Q = 2/3 closure, solvable for any member from the
   other two.

They meet at **strange** and at **charm**, each of which the
framework chooses to describe as a corner building with addresses
on both roads.

---

## The core: three sharp rungs

The spine of the framework is a sequence of Apollonian closures
on the lepton seed, each sub-percent or better:

```
  Q_3  =  2/3         (e, μ, τ)                     Koide 1981
  Q_4  =  1/2         + strange at k_4 = L          algebraic identity
  Q_5  =  1/3         + charm ≈ L³                   3D Soddy, 0.02%
```

### Q_3 = 2/3 — the Koide seed

Koide's 1981 observation (Koide 1983):

    Q_3  =  (m_e + m_μ + m_τ)  /  (√m_e + √m_μ + √m_τ)²  =  2/3

holds at ppm precision with PDG values (measured Q_3 = 0.6666605
vs 2/3 = 0.6666667). Given any two of {m_e, m_μ, m_τ}, the third
is determined by a Koide quadratic. The rotations:

```
  m_τ = K₊(m_e, m_μ)   =  1776.969 MeV   (measured: 1776.86)   +0.006%
  m_e = K₋(m_μ, m_τ)   =  0.510641 MeV   (measured: 0.510999)  −0.070%
  m_μ = K₋(m_e, m_τ)   =  105.6513 MeV   (measured: 105.6584)  −0.007%
```

Koide is not a contribution of this framework. It is the **seed**
the rest is built on.

### Q_4 = 1/2 — an algebraic identity, not an empirical hit

**This step is a pure mathematical consequence of the previous
one. It contains no new physics input.** Given Q_3 = 2/3 and the
specific choice k_4 = L for the fourth circle's curvature,
Q_4 = 1/2 follows as an identity:

```
  Q_3 = 2/3 means M_3 = (2/3) S_3²
  k_4 = L = S_3 · (1 − √(2/3))
  S_4 = S_3 + L = S_3 · (2 − √(2/3))
  M_4 = M_3 + L² = S_3² · [7/3 − 2√(2/3)]
  Q_4 = M_4 / S_4² = [7/3 − 2√(2/3)] / (2 − √(2/3))² = 1/2
```

Verified numerically (Q_4 = 0.499993 with PDG lepton values).
**The Q_4 = 1/2 closure is therefore a consequence of definitions,
not a fit against data.** No claim of empirical support attaches
to this step on its own.

The **physics-content** part of this step is separate, and it is
a one-line empirical observation: **the value k_4 = L corresponds
numerically to the strange quark mass at scale Σ_lep = 1.883 GeV**.

```
  m_s(Σ_lep) measured  =  95.02 MeV       (FLAG m_s evolved to Σ_lep)
  L² represented as     =  95.12 MeV
  residual                +0.10%
```

So strange has its address at ladder position 2, and that same
value also serves as the 4th tangent circle that closes the
lepton triple into the Q_4 = 1/2 Descartes relation. The 0.10%
residual is the empirical observation. The Q_4 = 1/2 closure is
a separate (and tautological) algebraic consequence of having
chosen k_4 = L. We separate these because the reader should not
double-count them.

### Q_5 = 1/3 — the 3D Soddy closure with charm

Adding a 5th sphere at ladder position 3 (integer) lifts the
configuration to 3D. Two distinct claims appear here, and we
separate them:

**Claim A (empirical, two residuals):**

```
  s @ Σ_lep:  L²  =  95.12 MeV    vs measured 95.02 MeV    +0.10%
  c @ m_b:    L³  =  927.66 MeV   vs measured ≈ 932.4 MeV  −0.51%
```

These are the empirical observations: that strange-at-Σ_lep is
near L², and that charm-at-m_b is near L³. Sub-percent and
half-percent respectively.

**Claim B (algebraic-geometric consequence of Claim A):** *given*
those placements, the 5-sphere Soddy configuration has

```
  Q_5  =  (M_4 + L³) / (S_4 + L^(3/2))²  =  0.333406
  1/3                                       =  0.333333
  residual                                     0.022%
```

The 0.022% closeness to 1/3 is **a property of the geometry that
follows from putting strange at L² and charm at L³**, not an
independent additional fit. It is what the 3D Soddy theorem says
should hold for 5 mutually tangent 3-spheres in this
configuration (Soddy 1936). The empirical content is in the L²
and L³ placements above; Q_5 = 1/3 is the geometric consequence
of those placements being structurally tangent in the
Apollonian sense.

This separation matters because a reader could otherwise read the
"0.022% closure" as a third independent empirical hit, which it
isn't.

**Charm's primary L-ladder address is therefore integer position
3, evaluated at the m_b scale**, with the 0.51% residual we
report and live with. It is less tight than the lepton Koide or
the strange identification, but it is the natural integer slot
and it sits inside the Apollonian-tangency condition.

## The two road systems

### Road 1: the L-ladder

```
  position n    particle           formula                      residual
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1/3           u                  L^(1/3) + δ_QED^u            +0.07%
  2/3           d                  L^(2/3) + δ_QED^d            −2.6%   (see below)
  2             s @ Σ_lep          L²                           +0.10%
  3             c @ m_b            L³                           −0.51%
  −7            Σm_ν               L^(−7)                       (contested — see below)
```

The u and d positions are **fractional iterations** of the
integer steps, with QED self-energy corrections (δ_QED^u and
δ_QED^d). The strange and charm positions are integer. The
neutrino sum sits in the negative direction — the extension
below the lepton seed — and is discussed under open problems.

**On the QED corrections:** δ_QED^u and δ_QED^d are 1-loop
electromagnetic self-energy contributions, evaluated in the same
scheme as the FLAG light-quark inputs (MS-bar at μ = 2 GeV). The
explicit formula and its derivation, the propagation of α and
charge-ratio uncertainties, and the comparison with isospin-
breaking lattice estimates are not yet written up in this
working document; this is one of the items flagged as a
prerequisite for any external publication. The numerical
contributions used here are δ_QED^u ≈ +0.10 MeV and
δ_QED^d ≈ +0.025 MeV at μ = 2 GeV. The u-quark agreement is
strong and the d-quark agreement is the 2.6% open problem
discussed below; the framework does not invoke any *additional*
correction beyond this single 1-loop term to close either gap.

### Road 2: the Koide triples

```
  triple                   Q       residual         evaluation scales
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  (e, μ, τ)                2/3     0.0006%          all at rest mass
  (c, b, t)                2/3     ≈ 0.001%         m_c at μ = m_τ,
                                                    m_b and m_t at self
```

The lepton triple is Koide 1981. The heavy triple is the new
structural observation in this work, in the spirit of (and
revising) the heavy-quark extensions attempted by Rivero (2011)
and others. Given any two members, the third is solvable via
Koide rotation:

```
  m_t from K₊(m_c(m_τ), m_b)  =  162.425 GeV   vs 162.5 GeV   −0.046%
  m_b from K₋(m_c(m_τ), m_t)  =  4.206 GeV     vs 4.203 GeV   +0.074%
  m_c from K₋(m_b, m_t)        =  1.144 GeV    vs 1.143 GeV   +0.126%
```

All three sit well inside current measurement uncertainty on the
heavy-quark inputs.

**The crucial structural input is that charm is evaluated at the
m_τ scale**, not at its self-scale. We want to be very clear about
what this is and isn't. We did not search across scales for one
that closes the relation and then report the closing one; the
m_τ choice has a structural story (m_τ is the heaviest lepton,
sitting just below the charm hadronization threshold at m_D, and
is the natural lepton-side endpoint of the L-ladder before the
hadronic continuum begins). However, *that story is not yet a
derivation*. We do not have an a-priori principle from which
"evaluate m_c at μ = m_τ" falls out. Until that principle is
found, the heavy Koide closure is best read **not as a confirmed
empirical result, but as a numerical puzzle**: there exists a
specific scale at which the (c, b, t) triple closes to Koide
Q = 2/3, that scale is the heaviest lepton mass, and we don't
know why. **Why m_τ specifically is the single most important
open theoretical question in the framework.** It is flagged as a
prerequisite for any external publication of this work (see
below).

For completeness: we did check neighboring evaluation scales for
charm. Self-scale (m_c) closes the triple to roughly 0.7% — about
two orders of magnitude looser than the m_τ evaluation. The m_b
scale closes to roughly 0.5%. The m_τ scale closes to roughly
0.001%. The closeness at m_τ is what makes the puzzle sharp; it
is also what makes the methodological caution above necessary.

### Where the two roads meet

Two particles end up with addresses on both road systems
simultaneously. Following the multiply-connected-address concept
introduced earlier, we choose to describe these as **corner
buildings**: same particle, two addresses, one per road.

**Strange** sits at L² on the L-ladder AND is the 4th tangent
circle that closes the lepton Koide triple into Q_4 = 1/2. As
discussed under the Q_4 section above, these are not two
independent observations — the Q_4 closure is the algebraic
consequence of the L² placement.

**Charm** sits at L³ on the L-ladder (at scale m_b) AND is a
vertex of the heavy Koide triple (at scale m_τ). The framework
chooses to describe charm as a corner building with two
addresses, one on each road. We do not insist on a global
geometric embedding in which both addresses sit cleanly on a
flat map; what we have is local validity at each address. The
same particle can be reached from either street, and on either
street the local description is correct — modulo the open
question above about why the m_τ scale is the right one for the
heavy-Koide road.

## Dimensional content: ratios, not absolutes

The framework's content is a system of mass **ratios**. For any
two particles X and Y with ladder positions n_X and n_Y:

    m_X / m_Y  =  L^(n_X − n_Y)

Both sides are pure numbers regardless of how L's units are
interpreted. To get absolute masses, anchor one (m_e in MeV) and
read the rest off as ratios. This is the same structure as
standard QCD: α(μ) is dimensionless, m_q / m_q' is dimensionless,
and one absolute mass (m_p or m_e) fixes the GeV scale.

Verified ratios (sub-0.5%):

```
  c(m_b) / s              =  L^1          9.753          (direct integer step)
  Σmν / s                 =  L^(−9)       1.253e−9       (if L^(−7) holds)
  s / u                   =  L^(5/3)      44.52          (with u from L^(1/3) + δ_QED)
  c(self) / c(m_b)        =  L^(π−3)      1.381          (the QCD running factor)
```

The last row is the QCD running factor for charm between the m_b
and self scales, expressed as a power of L. The exponent
**π − 3 ≈ 0.14159** is discussed under speculations.

Reading the framework as ratios instead of absolutes resolves
what looked like a dimensional ambiguity in earlier drafts. L
raised to any exponent is a pure number; multiplied by an anchor
mass, it becomes an absolute mass. The framework never asked
more than one anchor.
